refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks specificity on measurement boundaries (end-to-end definition), network assumptions, and the safety criticality context. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured from operator input to equipment response, bounded by network conditions, and directly supports safe remote operation—a high-criticality safety function.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrument teleoperation path with timestamped events at operator input, network ingress/egress, and equipment response; collect p95 latency under representative network conditions (agreed baseline); validate against 150 ms threshold under load and nominal conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire operator commands with minimal input-to-transmission latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The network link shall deliver teleoperation packets with bounded transit latency compatible with p95 ≤ 150 ms end-to-end.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The equipment shall process received commands and execute response with minimal processing-to-actuation latency.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report latency health to the tele-operator and safety services.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a dedicated low-latency teleoperation protocol stack with priority queuing, hardware-accelerated packet processing, and direct network link to minimize middleware overhead.
DP-B: Deploy a cloud-based teleoperation relay with edge compute nodes positioned near the worksite to reduce network hop count and introduce local buffering.
DP-C: Use a hybrid approach: direct low-latency link for critical control commands (steering, braking) via dedicated network channel; standard link for telemetry and non-critical tasking.
DP-D: Implement adaptive latency compensation via predictive command queuing and local autonomy fallback to mask network jitter.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid dual-channel teleoperation architecture with dedicated low-latency control link for safety-critical commands (steering, braking, emergency stop) and standard link for telemetry and tasking, combined with local autonomy fallback to mask transient latency excursions.

potential_sources: Coupling between control channel and telemetry channel: must ensure command-response consistency and avoid race conditions when switching between channels.
Coupling with Safety Services: emergency stop and safety alerts must be synchronized across both channels.
Coupling with Vehicle autonomy: fallback logic must not conflict with operator intent or safety constraints.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C decouples safety-critical latency (dedicated channel) from general telemetry latency (standard channel), reducing coupling complexity. However, channel synchronization and fallback logic introduce secondary coupling points that must be managed at lower decomposition levels.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports dual independent channels (or channel prioritization) under agreed conditions.
Assumption: Vehicle has sufficient local autonomy capability to safely execute fallback behavior during latency excursions.
Risk: Dual-channel architecture increases complexity and potential for mode confusion if operator is unaware of fallback activation.
Risk: Fallback autonomy may not align with operator intent in dynamic scenarios; requires clear operator feedback on active control mode.
Risk: Network conditions may degrade both channels simultaneously, rendering fallback ineffective.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly define: (1) channel selection logic and operator notification protocol, (2) fallback autonomy scope and constraints, (3) network condition thresholds triggering fallback activation, (4) synchronization mechanism between channels to prevent command duplication or loss.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication path including operator command acquisition, dual-channel transmission (control and telemetry), command processing, response execution, and latency monitoring. Includes protocol stack, channel management, fallback logic, and operator feedback mechanisms.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level mission tasks and operational directives from site manager or planning system; Teleoperation module translates tasking into real-time operator commands or autonomous execution parameters.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and latency metrics to monitoring systems; includes status of control mode (operator vs. fallback autonomy) and channel health.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and emergency directives from Safety Services; transmits safety-critical status and latency health alerts; coordinates emergency stop across both control channels.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Interfaces with physical/logical network infrastructure; manages dual-channel transmission, packet prioritization, and latency measurement; reports network condition status to Teleoperation logic.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own teleoperation implementation details. Teleoperation is a major subsystem that should be owned by a dedicated module.

Vehicle owns the physical equipment and local autonomy, but teleoperation is a cross-cutting concern that spans operator interface, network, and vehicle control. Assigning to Vehicle would diffuse teleoperation logic across operator-side and vehicle-side, violating cohesion.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for this FR. It owns the complete teleoperation path, dual-channel logic, fallback coordination, and latency monitoring. High cohesion, minimal diffusion. Clear ownership boundary.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, but not the teleoperation mechanism itself. Teleoperation must interface with Safety Services for constraint enforcement, not be contained within it.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP-C because it encapsulates the dual-channel architecture, latency management, and fallback logic as a cohesive subsystem. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, indicating strong module boundaries.
containment_risks: Risk: Fallback autonomy logic may require tight coupling with Vehicle module; if fallback behavior is complex, ownership may need to be revisited.
Risk: Network Link interface is critical; if network infrastructure is owned by a separate team, coordination overhead may increase.
Risk: SafetyAlert interface requires real-time synchronization; latency in safety alert propagation could undermine the 150 ms p95 guarantee.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has clear ownership of fallback autonomy logic and that Vehicle module provides a well-defined interface for fallback command execution. Establish SLA for SafetyAlert latency (e.g., p99 < 50 ms) to ensure safety alerts do not degrade overall teleoperation latency.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid dual-channel architecture) directly addresses the refined FR by separating safety-critical control (dedicated low-latency channel) from general telemetry (standard channel), enabling p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions. Fallback autonomy provides resilience to transient latency excursions. DP-C is feasible, testable, and aligns with high safety criticality.

diffusion_risks: Fallback autonomy logic may diffuse across Teleoperation and Vehicle modules if ownership is unclear; recommend explicit interface contract.
Network Link interface is a potential diffusion point; ensure Network Link module owns physical/logical channel management, not Teleoperation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module provides strong containment for DP-C. Dual-channel logic, protocol stack, and latency monitoring are cohesive within Teleoperation. External interfaces are minimal and well-defined. Fallback coordination with Vehicle is via SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces, maintaining separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: instrument teleoperation path with timestamped events, measure p95 latency under representative network conditions, validate fallback activation and mode switching. Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns end-to-end latency performance. Vehicle module owns fallback autonomy execution. Safety Services owns safety constraint enforcement. Network Link owns physical transmission. Ownership boundaries are distinct.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify channel selection logic, fallback activation thresholds, and operator notification protocol.
Establish SLA for SafetyAlert latency to ensure safety alerts do not degrade overall teleoperation latency.
Define interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle for fallback command execution.
Specify network condition assumptions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) under which p95 ≤ 150 ms is guaranteed.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed, DP-C is sound and addresses the FR, and Teleoperation module is the correct owner. However, DP-C introduces secondary FRs (sub-FRs 008.1–008.4) that require further decomposition to detail the dual-channel architecture, fallback logic, and latency monitoring. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to allocate these sub-FRs to lower-level DPs and IMs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire operator commands with minimal input-to-transmission latency (target: < 20 ms p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The network link shall deliver teleoperation packets with bounded transit latency compatible with p95 ≤ 100 ms under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The equipment shall process received commands and execute response with minimal processing-to-actuation latency (target: < 30 ms p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report latency health to the tele-operator and safety services, with alerts triggered if p95 latency exceeds 120 ms.

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports dual independent channels (or channel prioritization) under agreed conditions.
Vehicle has sufficient local autonomy capability to safely execute fallback behavior during latency excursions.
Operator is trained to recognize and respond to fallback mode activation.
Safety constraints and emergency stop procedures are compatible with dual-channel architecture.
Network condition baseline is defined and agreed upon (e.g., 4G LTE, WiFi 6, or dedicated private network).
open_risks: Fallback autonomy may not align with operator intent in dynamic scenarios; requires clear operator feedback on active control mode.
Network conditions may degrade both channels simultaneously, rendering fallback ineffective.
Mode confusion if operator is unaware of fallback activation; requires robust notification mechanism.
SafetyAlert latency may degrade overall teleoperation latency if not properly managed.
Dual-channel architecture increases complexity; potential for race conditions or command duplication.
evidence_needed: Network condition baseline and SLA (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, availability).
Vehicle autonomy capability assessment (fallback scope, constraints, execution latency).
Operator training and notification protocol specification.
Safety constraint compatibility analysis with dual-channel architecture.
Latency measurement methodology and instrumentation plan.
Prototype or simulation results demonstrating p95 ≤ 150 ms under representative conditions.
